title: Covert Malicious Finetuning Challenges in Safeguarding LLM Adaptation
abstract: Black-box finetuning is an emerging interface for adapting state-of-the-art language models to user needs. However, such access may also let malicious actors undermine model safety. To demonstrate the challenge of defending finetuning interfaces, we introduce covert malicious finetuning, a method to compromise model safety via finetuning while evading detection. Our method constructs a malicious dataset where every individual datapoint appears innocuous, but finetuning on the dataset teaches the model to respond to encoded harmful requests with encoded harmful responses. Applied to GPT-4, our method produces a finetuned model that acts on harmful instructions 99% of the time and avoids detection by defense mechanisms such as dataset inspection, safety evaluations, and input/output classifiers. Our findings question whether black-box finetuning access can be secured against sophisticated adversaries.
impact: We take several steps to mitigate the potential harms of this research. First, as previously discussed, we conducted these attacks through OpenAI’s external red-teaming program. Accordingly, we worked closely with them throughout our research process. We have also followed the conventions of responsible disclosure, giving OpenAI over 90 days notice before disseminating our results. Fortunately, our attacks are currently not possible to launch, as access to OpenAI’s GPT- 4 finetuning API is currently restricted to trusted commercial partners. For the long run, we believe that openly disclosing AI vulnerabilities is an important strategy for ensuring the development of safe AI systems. The vulnerability that we identify will exist whether or not we report it; by disclosing the attack early, we can ensure that future models can be finetuned in a secure manner.

query_input.scenario: Downloading community-shared quantized LLMs for local use.
query_input.issue: Quantized model hides malicious fine-tuning, activating harmful outputs after quantization.
hazard: Covertly embedding harmful behaviors via fine-tuning that evade safety evaluation, exploiting quantization or encoded triggers.
exposure: Developers deploying quantized models on edge devices.

hipporag_result.vulnerability: Developers lack detection methods for encoded malicious behaviors in quantized models, relying on safety evaluations that fail against covert finetuning.
hipporag_result.impact: Widespread harmful outputs on edge devices, endangering user safety and eroding trust in community-shared models.
hipporag_result.dose_response: Covert malicious fine-tuning, through developers deploying quantized models on edge devices, triggers undetected encoded harmful behaviors when safety evaluations fail, leading to widespread harmful outputs that endanger user safety and erode trust.

dose_response: Covertly embedding harmful behaviors via fine-tuning that evade safety evaluation, exploiting quantization, enables developers to deploy malicious quantized models on edge devices, which lack monitoring, leading to harmful outputs causing physical harm and safety incidents.
vulnerability: Community-shared quantized models lack provenance verification, enabling hidden backdoors to persist after compression.
Edge devices lack monitoring to detect anomalous outputs from compromised models, raising risk of uncritical acceptance.
impact: Harmful outputs cause physical or psychological harm to end-users, eroding trust in community-driven AI.
Deploying backdoored models on AIoT devices leads to safety incidents and reputational damage for developers.
